4: meizotera toutōn ouk echo charan hina akouo ta ema tekna en te aletheia peripatounta (meizoteran toutōn ouk echō charan, hina akouō ta ema tekna en tē alētheia peripatounta): 'I have no greater joy than to hear that my children are walking in the truth.' The elder's pastoral identity is entirely defined by the spiritual progress of his 'children' — those he has led to faith. Peripatountas en te aletheia (walking in truth) is the Johannine idiom for the whole-life embodiment of the gospel: truth is not merely intellectual assent but an ambulatory practice that shapes the whole life.
1: ho presbyteros Gaiō tō agapētō: 'The elder, to dear Gaius.' Presbyteros (elder) — the same self-designation as 2 John 1; used without further identification as though the title itself establishes authority. Agapētos (beloved, dear friend) — appears four times in 3 John (vv. 1, 2, 5, 11), saturating the brief letter with the affective vocabulary of covenant friendship. Hon ego agapō en alētheia (whom I love in truth): the prepositional phrase en alētheia modifies the quality of love — not merely personal affection but the love that exists in and is defined by the truth (the gospel, the covenant reality). Cf. 2 John 1: same construction; the Johannine love is always a love mediated by and consistent with the truth.
2: peri pantōn euchomai se euodousthai kai hygiainein: 'I pray that in all things you may prosper and be in good health.' Euodousthai (prosper, have a good journey, succeed) — the cognate euodia means good road/journey; the word-range includes material prosperity, business success, and the literal success of a journey. Hygiainein (be in good health) — the same word used in 1 Tim 1:10, Titus 1:13 for 'sound' doctrine, but here in its literal sense. The prayer reveals an integrated anthropology: the elder prays for the bodily health of Gaius alongside his spiritual flourishing — the well-being of psychē (soul) does not swallow the concern for bodily hygeia. This is the only direct prayer-wish for physical health in the NT.
3: emartyresan sou tē alētheia: 'testified to your truth.' Martyreō (testify, bear witness) — the Johannine tradition's signature verb for credible attestation (John 1:7-8; 3:26; 5:31-36; 1 John 1:2; 4:14; 3 John 3, 6, 12). The tesitimony is sou tē alētheia (your truth) — the truth as it exists in Gaius personally, an internalized reality verified by conduct. Hōs sy en alētheia peripateis (how you walk in truth): the present tense peripateis describes an ongoing observable pattern — 'walking in truth' is not a momentary decision but a verifiable way of life. The verbal report of traveling believers serves as a form of moral witness.
5: ho ean ergasē eis tous adelphous kai touto xenous: 'what you are doing for the brothers, even though they are strangers.' Xenous (strangers, foreigners) — those outside the immediate circle of known members; this is the ancient distinction between philos (friend, acquaintance) and xenos (stranger, foreigner). The commendation is specifically for Gaius's hospitality (xenodochein tradition: receiving strangers) to people he did not know personally. This was precisely the kind of hospitality that the Greco-Roman world expected of householders of standing; John commends it not merely as social courtesy but as pistos (faithful) conduct — a covenant term.
6: ous kalōs poiēseis propempsas axiōs tou theou: 'You will do well to send them forward in a manner worthy of God.' Propempō (send on one's way, speed on the journey) — a technical term in early Christian mission for providing a traveling missionary with everything needed for the next leg: food, funds, introductions to the next community, and possibly an escort. Acts 15:3, 20:38, 21:5 use the same verb. The standard axiōs is 'worthy': the provision should be at a level appropriate to the dignity of the one for whose sake they travel. Axiōs tou theou (worthy of God) sets the standard: the missionaries are God's envoys, and their treatment reflects the honor given to God.
7: hyper tou onomatos exēlthon mēden lambanontes apo tōn ethnikōn: 'For the sake of the Name they went out, receiving nothing from the pagans.' Hyper tou onomatos (for the sake of the Name) — to onoma without further specification is the Name of Jesus Christ, YHWH's eschatological Name (Acts 4:12; 5:41; Phil 2:9). The articularlessness is deliberate: any Jewish-background reader would recognize the Name as YHWH; the Christian community knows it is now the Name of Jesus. Mēden lambanontes apo tōn ethnikōn (receiving nothing from the pagans): the missionaries are self-supporting through the Christian community, maintaining independence from pagan patronage networks that would compromise the integrity of their message.
8: hēmeis oun opheilomen apolambanein tous toioutous hina synergoi ginōmetha tē alētheia: 'We therefore ought to receive such people so that we may be fellow workers for the truth.' Opheilomen (we are obligated, we ought) — a moral necessity rather than mere courtesy; the obligation flows from the logic of vv. 5-7. Synergoi tē alētheia (fellow workers with the truth) — synergos is Paul's characteristic term for missionary co-workers (Rom 16:3, 9, 21; 2 Cor 8:23; Phil 2:25; 4:3; Philem 1:24); John's use here makes the supporting householder a full participant in the missionary enterprise. By receiving the missionaries, Gaius becomes a co-worker not merely with them but with truth itself — a remarkable claim.
9: Diotrephēs ho philoprōteuōn autōn: 'Diotrephes, who loves to be first among them.' Philoprōteuō (love to be first, love pre-eminence) — a NT hapax. The compound philo-prōteuo echoes philargyria (love of money, 1 Tim 6:10) and philautos (self-loving, 2 Tim 3:2) in the vice-lists of the Pastorals: compounded philo- words typically describe pathological attachment to something. Diotrephes's disease is institutional primacy. Ouk epidechetai hēmas (does not receive/acknowledge us): the verb epidechomai can mean both 'welcome' and 'acknowledge authority of' — Diotrephes both refuses hospitality to the elder's envoys and repudiates the elder's authority.
10: phlyarōn hēmas logois ponērois: 'spreading malicious nonsense about us.' Phlyareō (talk nonsense, talk idly) — used only here in the NT; the cognate phlyaros (gossip, busybody) appears in 1 Tim 5:13. The term carries a connotation of idle, useless, and potentially malicious speech. Logois ponērois (evil words) — the content is not merely foolish but morally destructive. Oute autos epidechetai tous adelphous kai tous boulomenous kōlyei kai ek tēs ekklēsias ekballei (he himself refuses to welcome the brothers, and those who want to he prevents and throws out of the church): three escalating actions — refusal, obstruction, expulsion. Ekballō (throw out) is the word used for demonic expulsion and driving someone from a place; its use for Diotrephes's expulsion of hospitable members is pointed.
11: mē mimou to kakon alla to agathon: 'Do not imitate what is evil but what is good.' Mimeomai (imitate, model oneself on) — the mimēsis ethic is standard in the Greco-Roman moral tradition (pupils imitate masters) and in Paul (1 Cor 11:1: 'imitate me as I imitate Christ'). The imperative is addressed to Gaius and contrasts two models: Diotrephes (kakon) and presumably Demetrius (agathon). Ho agathopoiōn ek tou theou estin, ho kakopoiōn ouch heōraken ton theon: the dual verdict — the good-doer is from God; the evil-doer has not seen God. Heōraken (perfect tense: has seen) — the perfect implies that the evil-doer has never had the foundational vision that would produce good conduct.
12: Dēmētriō memartyrētai hypo pantōn kai hypo autēs tēs alētheias: 'Demetrius is well spoken of by everyone — and even by the truth itself.' Memartyrētai (perfect passive: has been testified to, is well attested) — the triple witness pattern: by all, by truth itself, by the elder. In Jewish law, two or three witnesses establish a matter (Deut 19:15; John 8:17; 2 Cor 13:1). John adds the personified truth as a witness — paralleling John 15:26 where the Spirit of truth bears witness to Jesus. The truth-as-witness formula elevates Demetrius's commendation beyond human social recommendation to a form of divine attestation.
13: polla eichon grapsai soi alla ou thelō dia melanon kai kalamou soi grapsai: 'I had many things to write to you, but I do not wish to write them with ink and pen.' Melanos (ink) — the standard black ink made from soot and gum. Kalamou (pen, reed) — the kalamos cut from reed, split at the tip, and used as a writing instrument. The decision to reserve fuller communication for personal presence rather than writing is consistent with the Greco-Roman epistolary convention of the letter as inferior to face-to-face speech; cf. 2 John 12 (almost identical phrase). The limitation is also practical: letters were read aloud in community gatherings, making sensitive matters about Diotrephes more appropriately handled in person.
14: elpizō de eutheōs idein se kai stoma pros stoma lalēsomen: 'I hope to see you soon, and we will speak face to face.' Stoma pros stoma (mouth to mouth) — see 2 John 12 note: the phrase echoes Num 12:8 (YHWH's speech with Moses 'mouth to mouth') and was a Hellenistic idiom for direct, unmediated speech. Eirēnē soi (peace to you) — the Jewish/Christian shalom greeting; its position at the close of the letter rather than the opening is unusual and signals the letter's relational warmth. Aspazontai se hoi philoi. Aspazou tous philous kat' onoma (the friends greet you; greet the friends by name): the use of philos (friend) rather than adelphos (brother) may reflect the Johannine tradition's language of friendship (John 15:13-15: 'I call you friends').
